bird.name: 黑頸鸛
bird.summary: 黑頸鸛（學名：Ephippiorhynchus asiaticus）是鸛科中一種頸長腿長的涉禽。這種留鳥分佈於南亞次大陸、東南亞及澳洲（孤立族群）。牠們棲息於濕地環境，常在稻田或麥田等農地覓食各類動物獵物。成鳥兩性皆具厚重鳥喙，體色為白色帶虹彩光澤的黑色斑紋，但雌雄可透過虹膜顏…

locations.name: Ashburton River (Western Australia)
locations.summary: The Ashburton River is located within the Pilbara region of Western Australia.

locations.name: Onslow, Western Australia
locations.summary: Onslow is a coastal town in the Pilbara region of Western Australia, 1,386 kilometres (861 mi) north of Perth.

relations.subject: 黑頸鸛
relations.evidence: |date=2019 |title=Confirmed nesting of Black-necked Stork Ephippiorhynchus asiaticus in lowland Nepal |journal=BirdingASIA |volume=31 |pages=88–90}} It extends into Southeast Asia, through New Guinea and into the northern half of Australia. Compared to other large waterbirds like cranes, spoonbills and other species of storks, black-necked storks are least abundant in locations that have a high diversity of large waterbird species. The largest population of this species occurs in Australia, where it is found from the Ashburton River, near Onslow, Western Australia, across northern Australia to north-east New South Wales

relations.evidence: |date=2019 |title=Confirmed nesting of Black-necked Stork Ephippiorhynchus asiaticus in lowland Nepal |journal=BirdingASIA |volume=31 |pages=88–90}} It extends into Southeast Asia, through New Guinea and into the northern half of Australia. Compared to other large waterbirds like cranes, spoonbills and other species of storks, black-necked storks are least abundant in locations that have a high diversity of large waterbird species. The largest population of this species occurs in Australia, where it is found from the Ashburton River, near Onslow, Western Australia, across northern Australia to north-east New South Wales

relations.subject: 黑頸鸛
relations.evidence: It lives in wetland habitats and near fields of certain crops such as rice and wheat where it forages for a wide range of animal prey
relations.object_summary: 浅水、沼泽与植被斑块组成的高生产力生态系统。

relations.subject: 黑頸鸛
relations.evidence: |year=1996 |journal=Wildlife Research |volume=23 |issue= 5 |pages=557–569 |doi=10.1071/wr9960557 |bibcode= 1996WildR..23..557H}} The largest known breeding population occurs in the largely agricultural landscape of south-western Uttar Pradesh in India. Densities of about 0.099 birds per square kilometre have been estimated in this region made up of a mosaic of cultivated fields and wetlands.{{cite journal |last=Sundar, K.S.G
relations.object_summary: 浅水、沼泽与植被斑块组成的高生产力生态系统。

relations.subject: 黑頸鸛
relations.evidence: |year=1988 |title=A summary of the larger waterbirds in Thailand |journal=Tigerpaper |volume=15 |pages=1–9}} Black-necked storks forage in a variety of natural and artificial wetland habitats
relations.object_summary: 浅水、沼泽与植被斑块组成的高生产力生态系统。

relations.subject: 黑頸鸛
relations.evidence: |first7=Gopi |date=2017 |title=Records of Black-necked Stork Ephippiorhynchus asiaticus from the coastal areas of the Kutch district of Gujarat, India |journal=Forktail |volume=33|pages=135–137}} In cultivated areas, they prefer natural wetlands to forage in, though flooded rice paddies are preferentially used during the monsoon, likely due to excessive flooding of lakes and ponds. Bird nests are usually on trees located in secluded parts of large marshes or in cultivated fields as in India and lowland Nepal
relations.object_summary: 浅水、沼泽与植被斑块组成的高生产力生态系统。

relations.subject: 黑頸鸛
relations.evidence: |first7=Gopi |date=2017 |title=Records of Black-necked Stork Ephippiorhynchus asiaticus from the coastal areas of the Kutch district of Gujarat, India |journal=Forktail |volume=33|pages=135–137}} In cultivated areas, they prefer natural wetlands to forage in, though flooded rice paddies are preferentially used during the monsoon, likely due to excessive flooding of lakes and ponds. Bird nests are usually on trees located in secluded parts of large marshes or in cultivated fields as in India and lowland Nepal
relations.object_summary: 具有较高含水量和密生植被的浅水环境。

relations.subject: 黑頸鸛
relations.evidence: |year=1991 |title=Notes on the behaviour and food-begging calls of a juvenile Black-necked Stork Xenorhynchus asiaticus |journal=Australian Bird Watcher |volume=14 |issue=1 |page=29}} Black-necked storks are largely non-social and are usually seen as single birds, pairs and family groups. Flocks of up to 15 storks have been observed in Australia and India, and these possibly form due to local habitat conditions such as drying out of wetlands. The black-necked stork is the type-host for a species of ectoparasitic Ischnoceran bird louse, Ardeicola asiaticus,{{cite journal |title=The species of Ardeicola (Phthiraptera-Ischnocera) parasitic on the Ciconiidae |last1=Kumar P
relations.object_summary: 浅水、沼泽与植被斑块组成的高生产力生态系统。

relations.subject: 黑頸鸛
relations.evidence: They are threatened by habitat destruction, the draining of shallow wetlands, disturbance at nests, overfishing, pollution, collision with electricity wires and hunting.{{cite journal|last=Clancy, G.P
relations.object_summary: 开发、采伐和围垦等活动导致适宜生境减少。
